card_text: Your liver processes food differently than you'd think.
caption: New research explores how diet — not just what's in your food, but how it's processed — may influence liver enzyme pathways linked to fatty liver disease risk. The science is still emerging (mostly early-stage and animal/lab research), but it points to a clear theme: whole-food patterns support liver function better than heavily processed ones. Another reason we keep ingredients simple and real. (Journal of Nutritional Biochemistry, 2026)
source_title: CYP450 Network Shifts in MASLD/MASH: From Pathogenesis to Nutrition-Informed Modulation.

card_text: Tradition on your plate: corn, beans, and real food wisdom.
caption: A new review looks at the Traditional Mexican Diet — corn, legumes, vegetables, and traditional techniques like nixtamalization — and its link to better metabolic markers and lower rates of certain cancers in studied populations. It's observational, not proof of cause and effect, but it's a compelling reminder: whole, minimally processed staples have always been quietly powerful (Plant Foods for Human Nutrition, 2026).
source_title: Preserving Tradition, Preventing Cancer: A Narrative Review of the Traditional Mexican Diet as a Framework for Cancer Risk Reduction.
source_journal: Plant foods for human nutrition (Dordrecht, Netherlands)

card_text: Maternal health today shapes tomorrow's met
caption: Maternal health today shapes tomorrow's met
source_journal: Journal of developmental origins of health and disease

card_text: Your cells are always listening to what fuels them.
caption: This review looks at how tumor cells reprogram glucose and lipid metabolism, especially under low-oxygen conditions, by altering RNA "tags" (m6A methylation) — not something diet changes directly. It's basic cancer biology, not a nutrition study. But it's a good reminder: metabolism is intricate and every cell responds to its environment. We just think that's one more reason to feed yours well. (Adv Exp Med Biol, 2026)
source_title: Influence of RNA Modifications on Cancer Metabolism: Interplay with Hypoxia and Therapeutic Implications.
source_journal: Advances in experimental medicine and biology

card_text: More plants, more variety, happier gut.
caption: A 6-week randomized trial with nearly 400 adults found that a diverse, whole-food plant-based diet was linked to improved gut microbiome composition, fewer digestive symptoms, and better reported energy and hunger levels compared to typical eating patterns. It's a solid reminder: variety in whole plant foods — not restriction — seems to be what our gut actually thrives on. (British Journal of Nutrition, 2026)
source_title: Does a diverse whole-food plant-based dietary intervention improve gut microbiome composition, gut symptoms, energy and hunger in healthy adults? A randomised controlled trial.

card_text: How you prep grains can change what your gut gets out of them
caption: New research on foxtail millet compared seven prep methods — raw, ground, steamed, extruded, and sprouted — to see how each affects nutrients and gut fermentation. Long sprouting (84 hours) stood out, boosting polyphenols and GABA while producing more beneficial short-chain fatty acids during digestion. One caveat: it also raised certain bacteria linked to potential risk, so more research is needed. A good reminder that how whole grains are processed matters as much as what's in them. (Food Research International, 2026)
source_title: Effects of different processing methods on the nutritional components and in vitro digestion and fermentation characteristics of foxtail millet (Setaria italica).

card_text: Cereal for breakfast? The science says: it depends on the box.
caption: New global research confirms what we've suspected: ready-to-eat cereal can boost fiber and micronutrient intake and support overall diet quality — but not all boxes are created equal. Researchers flagged ongoing concerns about sodium and added sugar in many popular options. Our take? Read the label, choose whole-food ingredients, and let breakfast do more for you. (Frontiers in Nutrition, 2026)
source_title: The nutritional and health benefits of ready-to-eat-cereal consumption: an updated technical review of global evidence.

card_text: Better detection tech means safer meat, sooner.
caption: Researchers refined a lab technique (PMAxx real-time PCR) that can spot live — and even hard-to-detect, dormant — Salmonella in retail meat more accurately than older methods. It's a food-safety science advancement, not a warning about your dinner. It just means testing labs get better tools to catch what matters. We're all for that kind of transparency (Journal of Microbiological Methods, 2026).
source_title: Detection of viable and VBNC Salmonella in retail meat using optimized PMAxx real-time PCR.

card_text: There's now a more precise way to catch dye in food.
caption: Scientists developed a new sensor using copper nanoclusters that can detect Sunset Yellow, a common artificial food dye, with impressive precision—even in complex food samples. It's a lab tool, not a warning label, but it points to a future where food safety testing gets faster and more reliable. We love seeing science make transparency easier to verify (Analytical Methods, 2026).
source_title: Rational design of L-histidine-stabilized fluorescent copper nanoclusters for highly selective and sensitive detection of sunset yellow.
source_journal: Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications

source_title: Repurposing apremilast for alzheimer's disease: multitarget modulation of cAMP‑PI3K/Akt-GSK‑3β and NF‑κB signaling.

card_text: Your brain's ketones might have a calming trick up their sleeve.
caption: New research suggests beta-hydroxybutyrate (BHB) — a ketone body your body makes naturally — may help calm brain inflammation by activating a specific receptor pathway (CB2R). The findings, from mouse and cell studies, are early but promising for understanding how metabolic states influence brain health. Not a treatment claim — just a fascinating glimpse into why nourishing your metabolism matters. (Journal of Physiology and Biochemistry, 2026)
source_title: Unveiling the role of CB2 receptor in beta-hydroxybutyrate mediated modulation of.
